MRI CERVICAL SPINE FINDINGS - DETAILED REVIEW:
No acute fracture or dislocation. Mild straightening of lordosis suggestive of spasm.
The MRI shows significant disc material extending into the neural foramen on the left side at the C5-C6 level. This directly
correlates with the patient's report of numbness in the thumb and index finger and the diminished brachioradialis reflex noted
during the physical examination. There is no evidence of spinal cord signal abnormality at this time.

X-RAY REPORT CORRELATION AND ANALYSIS:
Radiographic images of the cervical and lumbar spine were reviewed in the office today. Cervical films show a loss of the
normal lordotic curvature, which is a common finding in the setting of acute or subacute muscle spasm. There is no evidence
of acute fracture, dislocation, or spondylolisthesis. Mild degenerative changes are noted at the L4-L5 level which are likely
pre-existing but may be exacerbated by the recent trauma.

CLINICAL CORRELATION AND ASSESSMENT:
The objective imaging findings provide a clear anatomical basis for the patient's persistent radicular symptoms. The presence
of a confirmed disc protrusion at the same level as the clinical findings (C6) suggests a high likelihood that interventional
management will be necessary if conservative care continues to fail. We will monitor for any signs of progressive neurological
deficit which would necessitate more urgent surgical consultation.

ASSESSMENT AND TREATMENT PLAN
DIAGNOSTIC SUMMARY:
1. Cervical Disc Displacement (ICD-10 M50.20) with Radiculopathy (M54.12)
2. Lumbar Intervertebral Disc Displacement (M51.26) without Myelopathy
3. Post-Traumatic Myofascial Pain Syndrome secondary to MVA
TREATMENT PLAN DISCUSSION:
I have spent a significant amount of time today discussing the various management options with the patient. Given the
persistence of the radicular symptoms and the failure of initial conservative measures, I am recommending a Cervical Epidural
Steroid Injection (ESI). The goal of this procedure is to reduce inflammation around the compressed nerve root and provide
symptomatic relief. We will also continue with a structured physical therapy program focusing on stabilization and posture.
MEDICATION MANAGEMENT:
Patient is to continue Flexeril 10mg at bedtime for muscle spasm. Ibuprofen 800mg three times daily is recommended for
inflammation. We discussed the importance of taking these medications as prescribed to maintain therapeutic levels and
avoid rebound symptoms.

persistence of the radicular symptoms and the failure of initial conservative measures, I am recommending a Cervical Epidural
Steroid Injection (ESI). The goal of this procedure is to reduce inflammation around the compressed nerve root and provide
symptomatic relief. We will also continue with a structured physical therapy program focusing on stabilization and posture.
MEDICATION MANAGEMENT:
Patient is to continue Flexeril 10mg at bedtime for muscle spasm. Ibuprofen 800mg three times daily is recommended for
inflammation. We discussed the importance of taking these medications as prescribed to maintain therapeutic levels and
avoid rebound symptoms.
